# Get cluster-wide persistent/transient settings (verbose to see all)
kubectl -n elasticsearch exec elasticsearch-0 -- curl -sk -u "elastic:${PASS}" "https://localhost:9200/_cluster/settings?include_defaults=true&flat_settings=true" 2>&1 | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
# only show non-default differences
for section in ['persistent','transient']:
    s = data.get(section, {})
    if s:
        print(f'=== {section} ===')
        print(json.dumps(s, indent=2))
# show selected defaults that are interesting
defaults = data.get('defaults', {})
interesting = {k:v for k,v in defaults.items() if any(x in k for x in ['allocation','routing','rebalance','recovery','watermark','flush','shard','index.'])}
if interesting:
    print('=== relevant defaults ===')
    print(json.dumps(interesting, indent=2))
" 2>&1
input.description: Get cluster settings filtered to non-defaults and interesting defaults

apiVersion: v1
data:

Cluster: es-cluster
    \ |  Version: 8.11.1  |  Nodes: 3  |  Date: 2026-06-25
Auditor: automated read-only
    audit (no cluster changes made)
================================================================================

EXECUTIVE
    SUMMARY
-----------------
The cluster has a baseline security posture (TLS enabled,
    authentication
enforced, non-root pods). However, several high-severity gaps
    exist that deviate
from Elasticsearch hardening baselines and Kubernetes security
    best practices.
Remediation should be prioritised in severity order below.


SEVERITY
    LEGEND:  [CRITICAL] [HIGH] [MEDIUM] [LOW] [INFO] [PASS]


=== SECTION 1: AUTHENTICATION
    & AUTHORIZATION ===

[PASS] xpack.security.enabled = true  — Security X-Pack
    is active.

[PASS] Anonymous access blocked  — unauthenticated requests return
    HTTP 401.

[PASS] Password hashing algorithm: BCRYPT  — industry-standard adaptive
    hash.

[PASS] No active API keys  — _security/api_key returns empty set.

[PASS]
    No custom roles defined  — only built-in reserved roles are present;
       no
    over-privileged custom roles exist.

[PASS] action.destructive_requires_name
    = true  — wildcard index deletion
       (DELETE /*) is blocked.

[MEDIUM]
    DEPRECATED USER "kibana" STILL ENABLED
       The legacy `kibana` user (role:
    kibana_system) is enabled despite being
       deprecated since ES 8.x. Elastic
    recommends using `kibana_system` user
       exclusively. An enabled legacy account
    broadens the credential attack
       surface unnecessarily.
       Evidence:
    GET /_security/user/kibana -> enabled: true, deprecated: true

[MEDIUM] ELASTIC
    SUPERUSER PASSWORD IS WEAK / PREDICTABLE
       The `elastic` superuser password
    stored in secret elastic-password is a
       low-entropy, predictable string
    ("elasticpass-elastic-password-next").
       It contains no uppercase letters
    or special characters, follows a
       recognisable naming pattern, and appears
    to be a placeholder value.
       This is the most privileged account; any compromise
    grants full cluster
       control.

[MEDIUM] STALLED PASSWORD ROTATION SUSPECTED

    \      Two secrets exist: elastic-password and elastic-password-next.
       Both
    secrets currently contain the SAME password value. This indicates
       a rotation
    was initiated but not completed, leaving the cluster in an
       ambiguous credential
    state. The "next" password provides no security
       benefit if it is identical
    to the current one.

[LOW] run_as PRIVILEGE ENABLED CLUSTER-WIDE
       xpack.security.authc.run_as.enabled
    = true (default). This allows
       any user granted the run_as privilege to
    impersonate other users.
       Review all roles to ensure no unintended run_as
    grants exist.

[INFO] xpack.security.authc.token.enabled = false  — short-lived
    bearer tokens
       are disabled; only Basic and API-key auth methods are active.


===
    SECTION 2: TLS / ENCRYPTION IN TRANSIT ===

[PASS] HTTP SSL enabled: xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled
    = true
[PASS] Transport SSL enabled: xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled = true
[PASS]
    Negotiated protocol: TLSv1.3 (Cipher: TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384)  — 
       strongest
    available TLS version confirmed via openssl handshake.
[PASS] Ingress force-ssl-redirect
    = true  — HTTP->HTTPS redirect enforced.

[HIGH] TRANSPORT SSL VERIFICATION
    MODE: "certificate" (NOT "full")
       elasticsearch.yml sets:
         xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode:
    certificate
       This validates the certificate chain but does NOT verify the
    hostname.
       An attacker with a cert signed by the same CA can perform a
    man-in-the-
       middle attack on inter-node transport (port 9300) traffic.
    The baseline
       hardening recommendation is verification_mode: full.

[HIGH]
    INGRESS BACKEND TLS VERIFICATION DISABLED
       The NGINX ingress is configured
    with:
         nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-ssl-verify: "off"
       This
    means traffic from the ingress controller to the ES HTTP service
       is encrypted
    but the ES backend certificate is NOT verified. A rogue
       endpoint or pod
    could intercept that segment without detection.
       Recommendation: set proxy-ssl-verify:
    "on" and supply the CA cert.

[MEDIUM] SELF-SIGNED CA — NOT TRUSTED BY STANDARD
    CLIENTS
       HTTP cert:    Subject CN=es-http,   Issuer CN=es-ca     (self-signed
    CA)
       Ingress cert: Subject CN=es.example.com, Issuer CN=es.example.com

    \                    (self-signed, CA is the cert itself)
       Neither CA is
    a publicly trusted root. External or new internal clients
       must explicitly
    trust these CAs. Risk: certificate pinning bypass, MITM
       if the CA private
    key is not strictly protected.
       CA key is stored in Kubernetes secret es-http-tls
    (accessible to any
       pod with secret read permissions in the namespace).

[INFO]
    HTTP TLS certificates:
       Not Before: 2026-06-25 01:40:21 UTC
       Not
    After:  2027-06-25 01:40:21 UTC  (365 days)
       SANs: localhost, *.elasticsearch.svc,
    *.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local
       Note: SANs cover cluster-internal FQDNs
    but NOT external hostnames;
       external clients connecting via Ingress receive
    the ingress cert, not
       this internal cert.

[INFO] Ingress TLS certificate:

    \      Not Before: 2026-06-25 02:14:57 UTC
       Not After:  2026-06-25 02:14:57
    UTC (365 days)
       SAN: DNS:es.example.com only.


=== SECTION 3: AUDIT
    LOGGING ===

[CRITICAL] AUDIT LOGGING IS DISABLED
       xpack.security.audit.enabled
    = false
       No audit trail is produced for authentication events, access grants,

    \      access denials, configuration changes, or privileged operations.
       This
    is a primary compliance control required by SOC2, PCI-DSS, HIPAA,
       ISO
    27001, and most security baselines. Without audit logs:
         - Breaches cannot
    be detected or investigated.
         - Unauthorized access leaves no forensic
    trace.
         - Compliance attestations are unsupportable.
       The default
    event filters include ACCESS_DENIED, AUTHENTICATION_FAILED,
       and SECURITY_CONFIG_CHANGE
    — enabling the flag with default filters
       would immediately provide meaningful
    coverage.


=== SECTION 4: NETWORK EXPOSURE & POLICIES ===

[MEDIUM] NO KUBERNETES
    NETWORK POLICIES DEFINED
       kubectl get networkpolicy -n elasticsearch returns:
    No resources found.
       Every pod in the cluster (across all namespaces, depending
    on CNI
       configuration) can reach Elasticsearch on port 9200 and 9300. Lateral

    \      movement from a compromised pod anywhere in the cluster directly
       exposes
    the ES API and inter-node transport.
       Recommendation: define ingress NetworkPolicies
    restricting port 9200
       to known consumer namespaces and port 9300 to the
    ES StatefulSet pods
       only.

[LOW] ALL NODES BIND TO ALL INTERFACES (network.host:
    0.0.0.0)
       Combined with no NetworkPolicies, every node interface is reachable.

    \      Standard for Kubernetes pods, but noted as an amplifier for the
       missing-network-policy
    finding above.

[INFO] Elasticsearch is exposed externally via Ingress:
       Name:
    elasticsearch-ingress (namespace: elasticsearch)
       Host: es.example.com
    \ |  Class: nginx
       Backend: es-http:9200 (HTTPS)
       The ingress terminates
    external TLS (es-ingress-tls secret) and
       re-encrypts to the backend (proxy-ssl-verify:
    off — see Section 2).
       Port 80 traffic is force-redirected to 443.


===
    SECTION 5: CLUSTER SETTINGS ===

[MEDIUM] MEMORY LOCK DISABLED (bootstrap.memory_lock:
    false)
       Elasticsearch heap and index data can be swapped to disk. This
    degrades
       performance and, on unencrypted volumes, risks persisting sensitive

    \      indexed data in swap. For production deployments, memory locking should

    \      be enabled (requires ulimit -l unlimited on the host).

[MEDIUM] WATCHER
    SENSITIVE DATA ENCRYPTION DISABLED
       xpack.watcher.encrypt_sensitive_data
    = false
       Watcher action configurations (e.g., email credentials, webhook
    tokens)
       are stored in plaintext in the cluster state. If cluster state
    is
       exported or snapshotted, credentials in Watcher configs are exposed.

[LOW]
    AUTO INDEX CREATION ENABLED (action.auto_create_index: true)
       Any authenticated
    user with index write privileges can implicitly create
       new indices. This
    can lead to index sprawl and unintentional data
       landing outside governed
    index templates. Recommend restricting to
       known index patterns or disabling
    unless required.

[LOW] FIPS 140-2 MODE DISABLED (xpack.security.fips_mode.enabled:
    false)
       Required for regulated environments (FedRAMP, government, certain

    \      financial sector mandates). Note: enabling FIPS mode requires FIPS-
       capable
    JVM and certificate format changes; plan accordingly.

[LOW] HTTP RESPONSE COMPRESSION
    DISABLED (http.compression: false)
       HTTP response bodies are not compressed.
    While disabling compression
       is a mitigation against BREACH-style attacks
    (and therefore may be
       intentional), it increases bandwidth for high-throughput
    workloads.
       Document the intent if this is a deliberate security setting.

[PASS]
    action.destructive_requires_name = true  — noted also in Section 1.

[INFO]
    Cluster health: GREEN  (3/3 nodes active, 0 unassigned shards)
[INFO] Active
    shards: 0  (no user indices present)
[INFO] Max shards per node: 1000 (default)


===
    SECTION 6: NODE ROLES & TOPOLOGY ===

[LOW] NO ROLE SEPARATION — ALL NODES ARE
    master+data+ingest
       All 3 nodes carry roles: master, data, ingest.
       This
    is acceptable for small clusters but means master elections compete
       with
    data-intensive workloads. A split-brain or OOM event on a data-
       heavy
    node can destabilise cluster leadership. For production, dedicated
       master-eligible
    nodes (no data/ingest roles) are recommended for
       clusters expecting significant
    load.
       Current node roles:
         elasticsearch-0: [master, data, ingest]

    \        elasticsearch-1: [master, data, ingest]
         elasticsearch-2: [master,
    data, ingest]

[INFO] All 3 nodes healthy, transport reachable on port 9300.
[INFO]
    JVM heap: -Xms512m / -Xmx512m per node (resource limits: 2Gi RAM, 2 CPU)


===
    SECTION 7: KUBERNETES / POD SECURITY ===

[PASS] Pods run as non-root: runAsNonRoot=true,
    runAsUser=1000, fsGroup=1000
[PASS] Resource limits set: CPU 2 cores, Memory
    2Gi per pod.
[PASS] Readiness and startup probes configured.

[LOW] DEFAULT
    SERVICE ACCOUNT IN USE
       Pods use the `default` service account with no
    custom RBAC. If the
       workload node/pod is compromised, the service account
    token grants
       whatever the default SA is bound to. In environments with
    broad
       cluster-level roles, this can be significant. Audit default SA

    \      permissions for the elasticsearch namespace.

[LOW] CA PRIVATE KEY ACCESSIBLE
    AS A SECRET
       The es-http-tls secret contains the CA private key (ca.crt,
    tls.crt,
       tls.key). Any pod or service account with read access to secrets
    in
       the elasticsearch namespace can extract the CA key and issue valid

    \      certificates trusted by the cluster. Store CA private keys in an
       external
    secret manager or Vault rather than in cluster secrets.

[INFO] No container
    securityContext-level settings observed
       (readOnlyRootFilesystem, allowPrivilegeEscalation,
    seccompProfile).
       Consider adding these to reduce pod attack surface.


===
    SECTION 8: BACKUP & OPERATIONAL CONTINUITY ===

[HIGH] NO SNAPSHOT REPOSITORY
    CONFIGURED
       GET /_snapshot returns {}.
       There is no backup mechanism
    in place. A cluster failure, data
       corruption, or accidental destructive
    operation (e.g., index deletion)
       would result in permanent data loss.
    A backup strategy (S3, GCS, Azure,
       or shared filesystem snapshot repo)
    should be configured and tested
       before the cluster holds any production
    data.

[INFO] ILM policies: only built-in Elastic system policies exist.
       No
    user-defined ILM policies are configured.
       No data streams or user indices
    exist at time of audit.


=== SECTION 9: USERS & ROLES INVENTORY ===

Built-in
    users (all enabled unless noted):
  elastic          — roles: [superuser]              ENABLED

    \ kibana_system    — roles: [kibana_system]           ENABLED
  kibana           —
    roles: [kibana_system]           ENABLED (DEPRECATED)
  logstash_system  — roles:
    [logstash_system]         ENABLED
  beats_system     — roles: [beats_system]
    \           ENABLED
  apm_system       — roles: [apm_system]              ENABLED

    \ remote_monitoring_user — roles: [remote_monitoring_collector,
                                    remote_monitoring_agent]
    ENABLED

No custom users or roles defined.
No role mappings configured (external
    auth integration not in use).


=== PRIORITISED REMEDIATION CHECKLIST ===

Priority
    1 — CRITICAL (fix immediately):
  [ ] Enable audit logging: xpack.security.audit.enabled
    = true
      Add to elasticsearch.yml and rolling-restart the cluster.

Priority
    2 — HIGH (fix before production load):
  [ ] Change transport SSL verification:
    verification_mode: full
  [ ] Enable ingress backend cert verification: proxy-ssl-verify:
    "on"
      (supply es-http-tls CA cert to ingress via proxy-ssl-secret annotation)

    \ [ ] Configure at least one snapshot repository and test restore.

Priority
    3 — MEDIUM (fix in next sprint):
  [ ] Rotate elastic superuser password to a
    high-entropy random value.
      Complete or abandon the stalled rotation (elastic-password-next
    secret).
  [ ] Disable deprecated `kibana` user.
  [ ] Define and apply Kubernetes
    NetworkPolicies for the namespace.
  [ ] Enable memory locking: bootstrap.memory_lock
    = true
      (requires matching ulimit in pod spec / init container).
  [ ]
    Enable Watcher data encryption: xpack.watcher.encrypt_sensitive_data = true

Priority
    4 — LOW (planned hardening):
  [ ] Separate node roles: dedicate 3 master-only
    nodes + data nodes for scale.
  [ ] Restrict auto index creation: set action.auto_create_index
    to known patterns.
  [ ] Add pod-level security settings: readOnlyRootFilesystem,
    seccompProfile.
  [ ] Move CA private key to external secret manager.
  [ ]
    Create a dedicated service account with minimal RBAC for ES pods.


END OF
    AUDIT REPORT"
